svg.text: ANCIENT EGYPTIAN
PHONOLOGY — RECONSTRUCTING A DEAD LANGUAGE
EGYPTIAN PHONOLOGICAL RECONSTRUCTION
Hieroglyphs record only consonants; vowels unknown from writing alone
Reconstruction uses: Coptic vowels + Semitic cognates + Greek transcriptions
Result: approximate; Egyptologists use "school pronunciations" (Egyptological)
EGYPTIAN CONSONANT SYSTEM
~24 distinct consonants reconstructed from hieroglyphic evidence
Pharyngeals: aleph /ʾ/ and ayin /ʿ/ (like Semitic!)
Emphatics: /q/ and laterals • No Semitic shin; had /s/ and /š/
Unique: lateral /ɫ/ • alveolar /r/ • voiced fricatives /ɣ/
STAGES OF EGYPTIAN
Old Egyptian (~2600-2000 BCE): Pyramid Texts
Middle Egyptian (~2000-1300 BCE): classical form, hieroglyphic standard
Late Egyptian (~1300-700 BCE): colloquial forms emerge in writing
Demotic (~700 BCE-400 CE) • Coptic (~200-1300 CE): final stages
GREEK PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ANCIENT GREEK PHONOLOGICAL RICHNESS
Aspirated stops: ph /pʰ/ th /tʰ/ kh /kʰ/ (later became fricatives)
Pitch accent: each syllable had high, low, or circumflex pitch
Long vs short vowels: alpha, eta, iota each had long/short pair
Modern Greek: lost aspiration, pitch accent, and vowel length
GREEK-COPTIC PHONOLOGICAL INTERACTION
Coptic vowel system: directly from Greek vowels + Egyptian sounds
Coptic: 7 vowels (from Greek) representing Egyptian pronunciation
Coptic preserves Egyptian vowel sounds Greek alphabet could encode
RECONSTRUCTING THROUGH CONTACT
Greek transcriptions of Egyptian names: Amenhotep, Ramesses, Thutmose
Greek versions preserve vowels: e.g., Amenophis → Amun-hotep
Without Greek: Egyptian vowels would be entirely lost
Greek served as phonological key to unlock Egyptian voice across 4000 years
PLATE 61b ALT · ANCIENT EGYPTIAN / GREEK · PHONOLOGY
